authoritySource: Dr. Ahmed Hesham â€” OET Medical Vocabulary Rulebook for Healthcare Professionals v1

rules.title: Definitions medically accurate
rules.body: Definitions must reflect current medical usage (ICD-11 / current MeSH / national guidelines). Outdated terminology (e.g. 'senile dementia', 'mongolism') is forbidden.

good: Atelectasis â€” partial or complete collapse of a lung or a section (lobe) of the lung.
bad: Senile dementia â€” confusion in old age.

rules.title: No brand names as primary term
rules.body: Primary terms MUST use generic names (INN) not brand names. Brand names may appear as synonyms with the Â® stripped.

rules.title: Abbreviations expanded
rules.body: Every abbreviation term must include its expansion. e.g. 'ECG â€” electrocardiogram, a recording of the heart's electrical activity.'

rules.title: Healthcare register
rules.body: Entries must read as if appearing in an OET referral/discharge letter. No colloquialisms ('tummy ache', 'yucky'), no slang, no lay abbreviations.

rules.title: No trademarked names
rules.body: Never use trademarked product or device names as the primary term.

rules.title: Formal, non-judgemental
rules.body: Avoid pejorative terms ('drunkard', 'addict', 'non-compliant'). Use 'patient with alcohol use disorder', 'patient with opioid dependence', 'patient reported difficulty adhering'.

rules.title: Conditions lowercase except eponymous
rules.body: Diseases are lowercase ('hypertension'); eponymous diseases preserve the capital ('Parkinson's disease', 'Crohn's disease').

rules.title: Definition â‰¤ 25 words
rules.body: Definitions must be concise: target 12â€“20 words, hard cap 25 words. Use noun phrases where possible.

rules.title: No circular definitions
rules.body: A definition must not repeat the term it is defining. 'Analgesia â€” the use of analgesics' is forbidden.

rules.title: Example sentence uses the term in context
rules.body: The example sentence must use the term in a natural clinical sentence (OET letter register). Minimum 8 words, maximum 30 words.

rules.title: Synonyms genuine, not translations
rules.body: Listed synonyms must be true English synonyms used in UK/AU/NZ clinical practice. No translations into other languages; no false synonyms.

rules.title: Collocations 2â€“5 entries, realistic
rules.body: Collocations should reflect authentic clinical phrases ('administer medication', 'elevate the head of the bed').

rules.title: IPA in British Received Pronunciation
rules.body: When IPA is provided, use British RP conventions; mark primary stress with Ëˆ and secondary stress with ËŒ.

rules.title: Audio recommended for polysyllabic or Latin-root terms
rules.body: Terms with â‰¥3 syllables or Greek/Latin roots benefit from audio pronunciation.

rules.title: Canonical categories only
rules.body: Category must be one of: medical, anatomy, symptoms, procedures, pharmacology, conditions, clinical_communication, diagnostics.

rules.title: Difficulty tiered A2/B1/B2/C1 or easy/medium/hard
rules.body: Difficulty must be one of the documented tiers. Map A2â†’easy, B1â†’medium, B2â†’medium, C1â†’hard.

rules.title: Example sentence passes core OET grammar rules
rules.body: Example sentences must not violate G07.1 (no contractions), G07.2 (no 'the patient' as running label), G12.1 (translate Latin abbreviations).

rules.title: No second-person in examples
rules.body: Examples are third-person clinical narrative. Avoid 'you' unless directly quoting patient education dialogue.

rules.title: Medicine terms span core sub-specialties
rules.body: A balanced medicine bank includes general-practice, internal-medicine, surgery, paediatrics, obstetrics, and psychiatry bands. Avoid over-representing any one sub-specialty.
